verdictLines: The claim that plastic recycling is pointless presents materially opposing evidence. Historical recycling rates are very low (9% of 6.3 billion tonnes through 2015), supporting the pointlessness assertion. However, articulated environmental and resource benefits (landfill reduction, pollution prevention, emissions reduction) directly contradict it. Infrastructure constraints explain low outcomes without establishing inherent pointlessness. The categorical claim cannot be resolved as clearly true or false given this direct contradiction, yielding a MIXED verdict at 50% truth with 72% confidence.
## Verdict: Mixed Effectiveness of Plastic Recycling

The supplied evidence directly contradicts itself on whether plastic recycling is pointless. Historical outcome data shows very low recycling rates—only 9% of 6.3 billion tonnes recycled through 2015, with 79% diverted to landfills or released as pollution [EVI_001_RECYCLING_RATE_OUTCOME]—which supports the pointlessness claim. Conversely, articulated environmental and resource benefits [EVI_002_ENVIRONMENTAL_BENEFITS, EVI_004_RECYCLING_EFFECTIVENESS_ASSESSMENT] establish genuine purposes: landfill reduction, resource conservation, pollution prevention, and emissions reduction. Infrastructure and cost barriers [EVI_003_RECYCLING_INFRASTRUCTURE_LIMITATIONS] explain why current outcomes fall short but do not establish that recycling itself is pointless. The categorical claim requires that recycling has no meaningful purpose; the evidence shows purposes exist but implementation is constrained and outcomes are low. This unresolved tension prevents a clear true or false determination.
- Caveated sufficiency status indicates material gaps in evidence scope or source singularity that affect confidence in the verdict.
